prompt: "Generate a LinkedIn post expressing skepticism towards the use of LLMs (Large Language Models) in critical clinical tasks due to their unreliability. The post should reference an enlightening interview with Francois Chollet, who describes LLMs as 'memorization and pattern matching machines,' and their limitations compared to true intelligence, defined as the ability to adapt to an ever-changing world. Mention Piaget's similar definition of intelligence and Chollet's advocacy for hybrid systems combining LLMs with other approaches, like discrete program search, to emulate System 1- and System 2-type thinking as described by Kahneman. Invite real computer scientists to discuss or critique these ideas and point out that LLMs, while useful for drafting under clinical review, should not handle critical medical decisions. Agree with Chollet and Yann LeCun’s view that the solution is combining LLMs with more rigid, reasoning models to filter the outputs and prevent hallucinations, and cite Nabla's approach inspired by Ian Goodfellow’s Generative Adversarial Networks."
output:
Because LLMs are unreliable, I struggle to see how (or why) we will use them for clinical tasks that matter. This fantastic interview helped me see things more clearly. 

Francois Chollet explains that LLMs are memorization and pattern matching machines. While this is quite useful, it is not the same as intelligence, which he defines as “the ability to adapt to an ever-changing world.”

(Piaget similarly defined intelligence as “what you use when you don’t know what to do”).

Because memorization and pattern matching are limited, Francois argues for hybrid systems that merge LLMs/deep learning with other approaches (e.g., discrete program search) to, in essence, mirror what Kahneman described as System 1- and System 2-type thinking.

I'll let the real computer scientists elaborate or tell me why this is wrong. 

To me, the point is that if we ever broadly use LLMs for clinical tasks where errors matter, they will likely be combined with other types of AI. (Other leading computer scientists like Stephen Wolfram also argue for this). Spencer Dorn you are actually right. As Yann LeCun says, LLMs are "just" enormous autocomplete engines. When they are used to draft something, with systematic clinician review, that's OK. But we shouldn't use them for medical decisions when even 99.9% accuracy is not enough.

I agree with François Chollet that the solution is to combine LLMs (we should be careful with anthropomorphism but I like to think of them as the creative, subconscious part of the brain) with more rigid models (that would be the prefrontal cortex) that reason and "filter" the output of the LLM. Yann LeCun also suggests this approach.

At Nabla we are actually using such an approach to prevent hallucinations. It's inspired from Ian Goodfellow’s Generative Adversarial Networks, where two very different models compete against each other.

Use the example of generating a research report with researcher, writer, and quality assurance agents collaborating. output:
New agentic short course! Multi AI Agent Systems with crewAI, built with crewAI's founder and CEO João (Joe) Moura. In this course, you'll learn how to break down complex tasks into subtasks for multiple AI agents, each playing a specialized role, to execute. 

For example, to generate a research report, you might have researcher, writer, and quality assurance agents collaborate. You'll define the roles, expectations, and interactions between the agents—like a manager organizing a team.

You'll work with key agentic AI techniques like role-playing, tool use, memory, guardrails, and cross-agent collaboration. And you'll build your own multi-agent systems that can tackle complex tasks. prompt: Generate a LinkedIn post that explains the concept of planning as a key agentic AI design pattern, using a large language model (LLM) to autonomously decide the sequence of steps to accomplish a larger task. Use an example of an agent conducting online research to illustrate the approach. Mention the "ChatGPT moment" many experienced, and introduce the concept of an "AI Agentic moment" with a personal anecdote involving a live demo of a research agent pivoting from a web search tool to a Wikipedia search tool after encountering a rate limiting error. Include an example from the HuggingGPT paper that simplifies a complex task by decomposing it into steps using an LLM-generated plan. Acknowledge tasks that don't require planning and those that benefit from dynamic step determination. Provide recommendations to learn more, referencing specific papers: "Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models," "HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face," and "Understanding the planning of LLM agents: A survey."
output:
​​Planning is a key agentic AI design pattern in which we use a large language model (LLM) to autonomously decide on what sequence of steps to execute to accomplish a larger task. For example, if we ask an agent to do online research on a given topic, we might use an LLM to break down the objective into smaller subtasks, such as researching specific subtopics, synthesizing findings, and compiling a report.

Many people had a “ChatGPT moment” shortly after ChatGPT was released, when they were surprised that it significantly exceeded their expectation of what AI can do. If you have not yet had a similar “AI Agentic moment,” I hope you will soon! I had one several months ago, when I presented a live demo of a research agent I had implemented that had access to various online search tools.

I had tested this agent multiple times privately, during which it consistently used a web search tool to gather information and wrote up a summary. During the live demo, the web search API unexpectedly returned with a rate limiting error. I thought my demo was about to fail publicly. To my surprise, the agent pivoted deftly to a Wikipedia search tool — which I had forgotten I’d given it — and completed the task using Wikipedia instead of web search.

This was an AI Agentic moment of surprise for me. It’s a beautiful thing when you see an agent autonomously do things in ways that you had not anticipated, and succeed as a result!

Many tasks can’t be done in a single step. For example, to simplify an example from the HuggingGPT paper (cited below), if you want an agent to examine a boy's picture and draw a picture of a girl in the same pose, the task might be decomposed into two steps: (i) detect the boy's pose and (ii) render a picture of a girl in the detected pose. An LLM might be fine-tuned or prompted (with few-shot prompting) to specify a plan by outputting a string like "{tool: pose-detection, input: image.jpg, output: temp1 } {tool: pose-to-image, input: temp1, output: final.jpg}".

This structured output triggers software to invoke a pose detection tool followed by a pose-to-image tool to complete the task. (This example is for illustrative purposes only; HuggingGPT uses a different format.)

Admittedly, many agentic workflows do not need planning. For example, you might have an agent reflect on, and improve, its output a fixed number of times,  resulting in a set of fixed, deterministic steps. But for complex tasks in which you can't specify a task decomposition ahead of time, Planning allows the agent to decide dynamically what steps to take.

To learn more, I recommend:
- Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models, Wei et al. (2022)
- HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face, Shen et al. (2023)
- Understanding the planning of LLM agents: A survey, by Huang et al. (2024)

[Original text: https://lnkd.in/gM2ZWNsW ]

prompt: Create a LinkedIn post discussing the potential implications of California's SB1047 bill on open source AI models. Highlight the bill's impact on AI policy and its significance for those living in California. Emphasize how the bill could hinder open source development by sharing safety concerns purportedly leveraged by large vendors to stifle competition. Mention the insufficiency of the bill's carve-outs to address the ease with which model behavior can be modified with small amounts of training. Reference Jeremy Howard's article and include key points: the requirement for developers to assure that models do not possess hazardous capabilities, Senator Scott Wiener's insistence on open source compliance, and the impossibility of guaranteeing a released model's safety from harmful use. Conclude with a recommendation for regulating AI by focusing on its usage rather than development, and encourage sharing the post with policymakers. Use relevant hashtags such as #AI, #Policy, #California, #OpenSource, and #AIEthics.
output:
If you work in #AI #Policy or live in #California you may or may not know about the attempt to ban #OpenSource AI models in #SB1047.

This is another regulatory capture bill, where large vendors attempt to kill competitors by claiming safety concerns (Absurd of course: most #AIEthics work is done using open source models because researchers need the ability to inspect and modify the model). 

The California SB1047 bill will kill open source models in California. But legislators don't realize this because they've been told the carve outs in the bill are sufficient. Apparently the research community hasn't communicated how easy it is to change the behavior of a #LLM with small amounts of training. 

Jeremy Howard wrote an excellent piece on this here: https://lnkd.in/gisHcJ_A. The important parts are these:

* California’s SB 1047 bill requires, under penalty of perjury, the developers assure their “model has does not have a hazardous capability”, even if up to 25% of the weights are changed
* Senator Scott Wiener, the primary backer of the bill, says that they crafted the bill “in a way that ensures that open source developers are able to comply”.

and "It is not possible to provide assurance that a released model can’t be used to cause harm, because a released model can be changed."

The best way to regulate AI is like this:

"We can still regulate AI, and allow open source. To do so, we can regulate use of AI, rather than development of AI. If someone uses AI for election interference, or discriminatory resume screening, or deep fakes, for instance, that usage can be made illegal."

Please share with policy makers!

prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post that includes the following details:

1. Express excitement about taking a year-long break from work as a news junkie, emphasizing the timing of the break.
2. Mention a fascination with understanding decision-making, linking it to a career in journalism, a transition from being a lawyer, and a recent move into the AI business after earning an MBA.
3. Share that you served as Chief of Staff to DataRobot's COO-turned-CEO and took a break exactly one year ago without a specific plan.
4. Discuss being well-informed as a Chief of Staff but using newfound freedom to deeply immerse in current events and trends, reflecting on the future.
5. State that you turned 50 during the break.
6. List key takeaways from the past year, emphasizing AI's American origins, the importance of cooperation, collaboration, and transparency, AI's potential to solve pressing problems, and the effectiveness of women leaders.
7. Highlight the rapidly changing AI industry and pose a rhetorical question about what could pull you back into the AI field.
8. Identify two motivating factors: stories of AI improving lives and a LinkedIn message from a former colleague about an opportunity to work with Google’s first Chief Decision Scientist, Cassie Kozyrkov, now CEO of Data Scientific.
9. Announce your new role as Chief of Staff to Cassie Kozyrkov with a sense of fate and gratitude.
10. Use hashtags like #AI and conclude with a welcoming note from Cassie Kozyrkov.

Ensure the post is engaging and maintains a professional tone.
output:
What a time for a lifelong diehard news junkie to take a year-long break from work. What. A. Time. 

Especially when a lesser-included addiction of said junkie is, "How did this happen?” (Just try to guess what I'm referring to. You're all correct.) 

My need to understand how people make their decisions is one reason why, 30 years ago, I went into journalism; why, four years ago, I left a life as a lawyer and why, newly MBA'd, I picked the AI business because of its throughline in my career and its role in my own decision making as a leader. 

Scroll to the end for the punchline of how my hiatus year ends.  Stay for the story: 

After having served as Chief of Staff to DataRobot's COO-turned-CEO, I took a break exactly one year ago. I didn’t have a plan and didn’t yet want one. 

A Chief of Staff to a CEO must stay on top of the current events, so I was already well informed. But newly free to dig deeper, I've found myself more deeply immersed in current events and trends unfolding in the U.S. and around the world. From the safety of my own home, my Zoom password forgotten and my suitcase mostly unused, I’ve had lots of time to reflect, contextualize, and think about the future.

Oh, and also I turned 50.

A few takeaways from this chair in which I now sit:

- People have forgotten that AI was invented in America and that we need to lead on it.
- Cooperation, collaboration, and transparency always win. 
- AI is our greatest opportunity for rapid prototyping and solutioning for every single one of our most pressing problems. 
- Women are great leaders.

I've watched the AI industry change by the day, in broad and deep ways. It's a challenging world out there for all players. 

So, given all I know, what could possibly pull me away from this most awful hobby and plunge me back into the AI froth, you ask? 

Two things: No.1, the massive and growing collection of stories every day about AI improving someone's life, from finding a wounded soldier to predicting extreme weather to helping a quadriplegic person move a cursor. And Gen AI is upskilling people everywhere. There is hope to be had. 

No. 2, the LinkedIn message last month from a former colleague teasing the opportunity to work with the pioneer in the field of Decision Science herself, Google’s first Chief Decision Scientist, a voice that helped shape my thinking on getting value from AI and who’s been unstoppable since she broke out on her own last year.

AND SO, IT IS THEREFORE with shock and gratitude and a real sense of fate and fortuitousness that I hereby announce my next role as Chief of Staff to Cassie Kozyrkov, who today as CEO of her company Data Scientific helps executives and leaders all over the world make better decisions. 

I’m back! #AI It is with great pleasure that I welcome Karin Jenson as my Chief of Staff. Here's to a phenomenal partnership!

output:
[Cérémonie] 🎓 Ce soir, mercredi 26 juin 2024, ESIEE PARIS décerne son premier titre de Professeur Honoris Causa à Yann LeCun, diplômé de l’école, prix Turing, vice-président et « chief AI scientist » chez Meta. Yann Le Cun est également professeur à l’université de New-York.

En présence de Jean Mairesse, Directeur Général de ESIEE Paris, et Daniel NABET, Président du Conseil d'école ESIEE Paris, Yann Le Cun a été honoré pour avoir façonné l'avenir de l'intelligence artificielle avec ses travaux pionniers sur le deep learning. 

Ses innovations, dont les premières versions de l'algorithme de rétropropagation du gradient développées à ESIEE Paris, ont révolutionné la vision par ordinateur, le traitement du langage naturel et bien plus encore. Cette distinction marque un moment historique alors que ESIEE Paris célèbre ses 120 ans. 🌐✨

Recently, I met some incredible young women at Harvard Undergraduate Women in Entrepreneurship’s inaugural Female Founder's Circle. Talk about a powerhouse network! The passion and drive were contagious. 

#Entrepreneurship can be a wild ride, and fear of risk is a hurdle many #women face. But here's the truth I shared with these future leaders:

* You are your biggest obstacle. Believe in yourself!

* Persistence is key. If you have conviction, don’t take no for an answer. 

* Prioritize self-care. Leaders underestimate how contagious their energy is on their teams. It took me a lot of years to prioritize self-care and in doing so, positively affect those around me.

* Fortune favors the bold - I am a big believer in serendipity, but it is not accidental. Be in situations where you can create opportunities for yourself. 

* Surround yourself with the right communities and mentorship. 

The future for female founders is already here. Women are breaking down barriers. Excited for the next generation of women in #tech and #AI to lead.

output:
"Starting in high school or college, I have lived my life according to what I call Verbally Stated Goals (VSG). At each major juncture, I focus on the most core emotion at that time; I crystallize it into words forming a mission statement; and then I tell everyone I meet that this is what I am doing. Telling people my Verbally Stated Goal does two things: first, by declaring it in front of many people, this commits me to achieving the goal; second, when people know what I’m trying to do, they find ways to help me."
Adam Cheyer

prompt:
Generate a LinkedIn post that encompasses the following themes and details:

1. **Introduction and Future Vision**:
   - Imagine a world where a single person can achieve what used to take a team of ten software engineers. Highlight the impact of AI on creativity, efficiency, and productivity per person.
   - Mention this concept as one of the highlights from the Abundance360 Summit this year with Peter H. Diamandis.

2. **AI Evolution and Business Opportunities**:
   - Discuss the evolution of AI from powerful language models to "large action models" that can turn ideas into reality.
   - Emphasize that there's never been a better time to start a company.

3. **AI in Science and Biology**:
   - Explain how AI will revolutionize fields like science and biology, including drug discovery and therapeutics.
   - Mention that AI can significantly reduce the average 17-year timeline for scientific discoveries to reach physicians, as it can process scientific discoveries within minutes.
   - Highlight AI's potential applications in the discovery of new materials, chemistry, and physics.

4. **Virtual Human Economy**:
   - Share your experience of hosting a session with Natalie Monbiot on the emerging virtual human economy.
   - Provide examples of people using their AI selves to earn passive income, such as teaching online lectures in languages they don't speak.
   - Mention celebrities licensing their voices to video games.
   - Discuss the risks associated with AI cloning technologies, such as retaining control over virtual identities, detecting misuse, and protecting against it with the hashtag #claimyourclone.

5. **Youth Participation and Personal Note**:
   - Express pride in your son Adam, who facilitated the teen program at the summit, and discuss how youths are using AI in their daily lives and to achieve their goals.
   - Describe visiting Machani Robots and the children's comfort with these social robots, including a specific instance where a robot sang "Shake it Off" by Taylor Swift.

6. **Key Takeaways and Inspirational Stories**:
   - Highlight the key takeaway from Abundance360: be BOLD and relentless in pursuing dreams.
   - Share inspirational stories from the summit:
     - Jason Ballard's vision of tackling poverty with 3D-printed houses.
     - Dean Kamen’s mission to unite kids globally in an annual robotics competition and its emotional impact.
   
7. **Conclusion**:
   - Convey that the summit was not just about the future but about the incredible humans shaping it, making it a future worth getting excited about.
output:
Imagine a world where a single person can achieve what used to take a team of ten software engineers. That's the future AI is ushering in, with an exposition in creativity, efficiency and productivity on a per person basis… one of the highlights from the Abundance360 Summit this year Peter H. Diamandis. 

There’s never been a better time to start a company as AI evolves from powerful language models to "large action models" that can translate ideas into reality. 

But it doesn’t stop there. #AI will revolutionize science and biology, including drug discovery and therapeutics. Today, it takes on average 17 years for a scientific discovery to make its way to physicians. Well, AI can hugely reduce this timeline as it can ingest scientific discoveries in a matter of minutes. I am also excited for the application of AI in the discovery of new material, chemistry and physics. 

At the summit, I loved hosting a session with Natalie Monbiot on the emerging virtual human economy. We showed examples of people putting their AI self to work to earn passive income. For instance, your AI self could teach online lectures, and do so in a language you don’t even speak in real life! 

Celebrities are at the forefront of this, licensing their voice to video games. Of course, while these cloning technologies can be empowering, they also present risks: how do you retain control over your virtual identity? How can we detect misuse, and how can we protect against it? In short: How do we make AI cloning work for us and not against us? #claimyourclone 

I’m also extremely proud of my son Adam, who rocked facilitating the teen program this year, bringing youth voices into the mix. I even got to hang out at their table! We explored ways these young people are using AI in their daily lives, and how they plan to use AI to achieve their goals. 

We also had the opportunity to visit Machani Robots. As adults, we often get trapped in the uncanny valley of these robots. But kids? They engage so comfortably with these social robots. We even got Ria to sing “Shake it off” by Taylor Swift. 

But the biggest takeaway I brought home from Abundance 360: be BOLD. Be relentless in pursuit of your dreams. Jason Ballard, the founder of ICON, left me speechless with his vision of tackling poverty with 3D-printed houses – beautiful, affordable, and built in record time. Then there was Dean Kamen, the inventor behind the Segway and the first insulin pump for diabetics. He brought the entire room to tears with his mission to unite kids from across the globe in an annual robotics competition.

prompt:
Please generate a LinkedIn post with the following details:

---

**Content:**

This revolution we're in... is it an #AI revolution? #UX revolution? Both?

I like this attached Garry Tan tweet a lot. 

To explain why, let's take a quick tour of recent AI hype cycles:

AI buzz is, of course, not new! Two decades ago, hype was high. But most of the chatter involved aspirational methodology — theory that hadn’t been put into practice yet — because two components were still missing: huge datasets and the computer hardware to process them.

In ~2016, AI was also everywhere. But it was hidden in plain sight as a subtle, unobtrusive component in software applications. Netflix didn't want you thinking about its underlying AI. They wanted to serve you the most accurate recommendations possible tp keep you watching longer.

Now... today. ChatGPT launched November '22 and suddenly the user was encouraged to interact directly with an AI component. We moved from an era of AI delivering seamless correctness, to an era in which AI is designed for usefulness.

Behind the scenes, UX designers are permitting themselves to serve you an unprecedented user interaction: an AI system that’s allowed to serve garbage… as long as it’s useful garbage.

What kicked off the UX shift? Public tools for generating AI art. Since art is in the eye of the beholder, that conversation was already more about usefulness rather than correctness ("Is that a hand with 7 fingers looool” but also “maybe if I rerun the prompt a few dozen times, it'll be cool enough to use in my blog post.")

Then ChatGPT leveled things up by offering the same incorrect-but-useful experience to the nontechnical public in what has been the most popular user interaction for decades: text-in-text-out. The result is a massive reset in user expectations about what “quality” means for AI output. Remember, GPT (the core tech) models were available several years before ChatGPT (the interface that let non-programmers interact with the core tech easily). The change came from allowing users to interact. In doing so, it altered user expectations. 

So what does this mean going forward?

For better or worse, many decisions related to AI are shifting from the AI builders to the users themselves. Great UX will be essential; tools must be easy, elegant, and intuitive to attract more users.

But UX is not just about ease of use. It's about how much control we have. It's about building a steering wheel so we AI users can better control the rapidly-evolving technology that the AI builders are shipping. I write more about building control systems here: https://lnkd.in/e-9iPWxY

Are you thinking about AI UX? Or AI control? I'd love to hear your thoughts - how do you see it evolving? And what do you want to see next?

---

Use appropriate LinkedIn formatting including hashtags, mentions if applicable, and links. Make the post engaging and conversational to encourage comments and interaction.

prompt: Write a LinkedIn post describing four design patterns for AI agentic workflows that can drive significant progress. These patterns should include Reflection, Tool use, Planning, and Multi-agent collaboration. Emphasize that instead of having an LLM generate its final output directly, an agentic workflow prompts the LLM multiple times to build step by step to higher-quality output. Focus particularly on Reflection, explaining that it is quick to implement and can lead to surprising performance gains. Illustrate this by describing a scenario where an LLM writes code, reflects on its own output, receives constructive criticism, and rewrites the code to improve it. Highlight the benefits of automating the step of delivering critical feedback for producing better responses. Mention the usefulness of tools like unit tests or web searches for evaluating output, and discuss the potential of using a multi-agent framework for further improvement. Conclude with references to three publications about reflection: "Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback" by Madaan et al. (2023), "Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning" by Shinn et al. (2023), and "CRITIC: Large Language Models Can Self-Correct with Tool-Interactive Critiquing" by Gou et al. (2024).
output:
Last week, I described four design patterns for AI agentic workflows that I believe will drive significant progress: Reflection, Tool use, Planning and Multi-agent collaboration. Instead of having an LLM generate its final output directly, an agentic workflow prompts the LLM multiple times, giving it opportunities to build step by step to higher-quality output. Here, I'd like to discuss Reflection. It's relatively quick to implement, and I've seen it lead to surprising performance gains. 

You may have had the experience of prompting ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini, receiving unsatisfactory output, delivering critical feedback to help the LLM improve its response, and then getting a better response. What if you automate the step of delivering critical feedback, so the model automatically criticizes its own output and improves its response? This is the crux of Reflection. 

Take the task of asking an LLM to write code. We can prompt it to generate the desired code directly to carry out some task X. Then, we can prompt it to reflect on its own output, perhaps as follows:

Here’s code intended for task X:
[previously generated code]
Check the code carefully for correctness, style, and efficiency, and give constructive criticism for how to improve it.

Sometimes this causes the LLM to spot problems and come up with constructive suggestions. Next, we can prompt the LLM with context including (i) the previously generated code and (ii) the constructive feedback, and ask it to use the feedback to rewrite the code. This can lead to a better response. Repeating the criticism/rewrite process might yield further improvements. This self-reflection process allows the LLM to spot gaps and improve its output on a variety of tasks including producing code, writing text, and answering questions.

And we can go beyond self-reflection by giving the LLM tools that help evaluate its output; for example, running its code through a few unit tests to check whether it generates correct results on test cases or searching the web to double-check text output. Then it can reflect on any errors it found and come up with ideas for improvement.

Further, we can implement Reflection using a multi-agent framework. I've found it convenient to create two agents, one prompted to generate good outputs and the other prompted to give constructive criticism of the first agent's output. The resulting discussion between the two agents leads to improved responses.

Reflection is a relatively basic type of agentic workflow, but I've been delighted by how much it improved my applications’ results. If you’re interested in learning more about reflection, I recommend:
- Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback, by Madaan et al. (2023)
- Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning, by Shinn et al. (2023)
- CRITIC: Large Language Models Can Self-Correct with Tool-Interactive Critiquing, by Gou et al. (2024)

[Original text: https://lnkd.in/g4bTuWtU ]

prompt: Reflect on a significant career learning experience related to open source. For instance, discuss software you developed that wasn’t open-sourced but had great potential. Then, highlight recent achievements in open-sourcing similar software at your current company and express excitement about the possibilities it unlocks for the wider community. Conclude with personal thoughts on the importance of open-sourcing and how it aligns with your current role.
output:
One of my biggest learnings from my career at Microsoft was that we should have open-sourced more of the work that my team did there. We had an internal C++ library called VisionTools that was a building block for lots of great products: Photosynth, Windows Photo Gallery, and Kinect to name a few. The library currently sits barely used now in some internal repository. I'm convinced that it could have instead powered many more projects out in the open world. But that was a different time and luckily the industry now has many more open-source proponents.

So, I'm thrilled that Ocean, the core image-processing and CV C++ library that we use at Meta, is now open-source. This library was specifically engineered to meet the requirements of building apps on mobile devices. It has a small binary footprint, is optimized for mobile processors, and has some nice examples to get started quickly with things like processing a live camera feed. Ocean is used across all of Meta's apps (Facebook, IG, Messenger, WhatsApp) as well as the Quest VR headsets. I'm excited to see what people build with it! Yes, nothing more frustrating than seeing an awesome piece of software you contributed to sitting on a digital shelf, gathering digital dust, ignored, because your employers doesn't believe in open source.
I suffered from this at AT&T.
One big reason I'm at Meta is that open sourcing infrastructure software and research code is in the DNA of the company.

prompt: Please write a LinkedIn post discussing the inclusive nature of the machine learning field. Highlight how the field has accepted various algorithms, even those developed long before the invention of computers, unlike the initial reaction from statistics departments to machine learning developments. Use this contrast to lead into a discussion about "agentic systems" and how we should view systems as being "agent-like" to varying degrees instead of debating their classification as true agents. Emphasize the importance of fostering a welcoming environment for everyone interested in working on agentic systems and the substantive value the term “agentic” has in technical discussions as opposed to marketing jargon. Finally, encourage a collaborative and inclusive approach in building increasingly sophisticated agentic workflows.
output:
One reason for machine learning’s success is that our field welcomes a wide range of work. I can’t think of even one example where someone developed what they called a machine learning algorithm and senior members of our community criticized it saying, “that’s not machine learning!” Indeed, linear regression using a least-squares cost function was used by mathematicians Legendre and Gauss in the early 1800s — long before the invention of computers — yet machine learning has embraced these algorithms, and we routinely call them “machine learning” in introductory courses!

In contrast, about 20 years ago, I saw statistics departments at a number of universities look at developments in machine learning and say, “that’s not really statistics.” This is one reason why machine learning grew much more in computer science than statistics departments. (Fortunately, since then, most statistics departments have become much more open to machine learning.)

This contrast came to mind a few months ago, as I thought about how to talk about agentic systems that use design patterns such as reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent collaboration t0 produce better results than zero-shot prompting. I had been involved in conversations about whether certain systems should count as “agents.” Rather than having to choose whether or not something is an agent in a binary way, I thought, it would be more useful to think of systems as being agent-like to different degrees. Unlike the noun “agent,” the adjective “agentic” allows us to contemplate such systems and include all of them in this growing movement.

More and more people are building systems that prompt a large language model multiple times using agent-like design patterns. But there’s a gray zone between what clearly is not an agent (prompting a model once) and what clearly is (say, an autonomous agent that, given high-level instructions, plans, uses tools, and carries out multiple, iterative steps of processing).

Rather than arguing over which work to include or exclude as being a true agent, we can acknowledge that there are different degrees to which systems can be agentic. Then we can more easily include everyone who wants to work on agentic systems. We can also encourage newcomers to start by building simple agentic workflows and iteratively make their systems more sophisticated.

In the past few weeks, I’ve noticed that, while technical people and non-technical people alike sometimes use the word “agent,” mainly only technical people use the word “agentic” (for now!). So when I see an article that talks about “agentic” workflows, I’m more likely to read it, since it’s less likely to be marketing fluff and more likely to have been written by someone who understands the technology.

Let’s keep working on agentic systems and keep welcoming anyone who wants to join our field!

[Original text: https://lnkd.in/gErMEPZC ]

prompt: Generate a LinkedIn post about the emergence of multi-agent collaboration as a key AI agentic design pattern. The post should explain how complex tasks, like writing software, are broken down into subtasks to be executed by different roles (e.g., software engineer, product manager, designer, quality assurance engineer). Each role is represented by different agents built by prompting a Large Language Model (LLM). Highlight why using multiple agents is effective, such as improved performance demonstrated by ablation studies and better handling of long, complex inputs. Discuss how this approach mirrors real-world team management and the benefits of an agentic workflow. Mention the role of multi-agent frameworks like AutoGen, Crew AI, and LangGraph, and encourage readers to experiment with ChatDev, a virtual software company implementation. Conclude with recommendations for further reading on the topic, citing relevant papers (e.g., Communicative Agents for Software Development, AutoGen, MetaGPT). Include a link to the original text if necessary.
output:
Multi-agent collaboration has emerged as a key AI agentic design pattern. Given a complex task like writing software, a multi-agent approach would break down the task into subtasks to be executed by different roles -- such as a software engineer, product manager, designer, quality assurance engineer, and so on -- and have different agents accomplish different subtasks.

Different agents might be built by prompting a LLM to carry out different tasks. For example, to build a software engineer agent, we might prompt: "You are an expert in writing clear, efficient code. Write code to perform the task …". 

It might seem counterintuitive that, although we are making multiple calls to the same LLM, we apply the programming abstraction of using multiple agents. I'd like to offer a few reasons:
- It works! Many teams are getting good results with this method, and there's nothing like results! Ablation studies (for example, in the AutoGen paper cited below) show that multiple agents give superior performance to a single agent. 
- Even though some LLMs today can accept very long input contexts, their ability to truly understand long, complex inputs is mixed. An agentic workflow in which the LLM is prompted to focus on one thing at a time can give better performance. 
- It gives us a framework for breaking down complex tasks. When writing code to run on a single CPU, we often break our program up into different processes or threads. This lets us decompose a task -- like implementing a web browser -- into subtasks that are easier to code. Multi-agents roles is, similarly, a useful abstraction.

In companies, managers decide what roles to hire and then how to split complex projects into smaller tasks to assign to employees with different specialties. Using multiple agents is analogous. Each agent implements its own workflow, has its own memory (itself a rapidly evolving area in agentic technologies -- how can an agent remember enough of its past interactions to perform better on upcoming ones?), and may ask other agents for help. Agents themselves can also engage in Planning and Tool Use. While managing people is hard, it's a sufficiently familiar idea that it gives us a mental framework for how to "hire" and assign tasks to our AI agents. 

Frameworks like AutoGen, Crew AI, and LangGraph provide rich ways to build multi-agent solutions. If you're interested in playing with a fun multi-agent system, check out ChatDev, an open source implementation of a set of agents that run a virtual software company. While it may not always produce what you want, you might be amazed at how well it does! 

To learn more, I recommend: 
- Communicative Agents for Software Development, Qian et al. (2023) (the ChatDev paper)
- AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation, Wu et al. (2023) 
- MetaGPT: Meta Programming for A Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework, Hong et al. (2023) 

[Original text: https://lnkd.in/g7gShRnf ]

prompt: "Write a LinkedIn post expressing support for the Washington Post's editorial board's push for governments to explore climate geoengineering. Emphasize the role of AI climate modeling in this context. Mention the projected warming trajectory, the effectiveness of stratospheric aerosol injection (SAI) in cooling the Earth, and the current limitations in our understanding of its local climate impacts. Highlight the importance of better AI models to reduce uncertainties and briefly touch on additional considerations such as governance, moral hazard, equity, pollution, and implementation challenges."
output:
I'm glad the Washington Post's editorial board is pushing for governments to engage in exploring climate geoengineering. I believe AI climate modeling has an important role to play. 

Here's the situation as I see it:
- Earth is on track to a catastrophic 2-4 degrees Celsius of warming. The article references the UN Environment Program's estimate of 2.9 degrees on the current trajectory.
- We have a high degree of confidence that geoengineering via stratospheric aerosol injection (SAI) will significantly lower Earth's average surface temperature. The science here is really solid: Use aerosols in the atmosphere to reflect more sunlight away from earth, and we become cooler.
- So, why don't we just do it? Multiple reasons, but the biggest is that we still don't have good models for estimating how it will affect local climate and weather patterns, even though we're confident global average surface temperature will go down. 

That's why better AI climate modeling is crucial for reducing uncertainty and better understanding the impacts of various geoengineering strategies.

There're other issues to consider too, like governance, moral hazard (disincentivizing decarbonization), equity,  pollution from the aerosols, and the challenging implementation engineering. But many of these  problems become easier if we can make progress on the core problem of better understanding what impact SAI will have.

prompt:
"Generate a LinkedIn post about the experience of using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) with the Dexcom 7 sensor and the Levels app. In the post, you should:

1. Describe your background as a data enthusiast and investor with Blue Tulip Ventures, including your work at Affectiva.
2. Express your belief in the revolutionary potential of new sensors, data, and AI to democratize access to health and wellness.
3. Share seven learnings from using a CGM, touching on mindful eating, the benefits of post-meal walks, mindfulness in snacking, the impact of vitamin gummies, travel challenges, form factor improvements, and the potential for multimodal integration.

End the post by inviting others to share their experiences and suggestions related to CGMs and #human sensing startups. Use a friendly and engaging tone, and include relevant emojis to make the post more visually appealing."
output:
I am a data geek and a big believer that new sensors, data and AI will revolutionize and democratize access to health and wellness! Human sensing AI is one of the investment themes of Blue Tulip Ventures. At Affectiva, I spent 20 years doing external sensing of human signals, now I’m turning that inward!

For the past couple of months I’ve been experimenting with a continuous glucose monitor (CGM). I use Dexcom 7 and the Levels app and have found the data to be fascinating. The app tracks the immediate effect foods (and activity) has on your glucose level, which is super cool. 

7 things I learnt from wearing a continuous glucose monitor: 

1- Mindful eating works: when I’m intentional about my food, starting every meal with veggies and fiber 🥦🥬🥑, then proteins 🐟 and then optionally end with a some fruit or choc (if I am craving that), my glucose doesn’t spike at all. Thank you GlucoseGoddess for that tip. 

2- Take that walk after your meals: A 30-45 minute walk after dinner really helps level my glucose level (Thank you Sophie V. Vandebroek for that tip!) 

3- Watch your snack! The way I snack is the opposite of mindful eating 😫🙈. I basically sit on my laptop and munch away! And it turns out that a spike from a handful or grapes or raspberries is equal to that of eating 3-4 squares of dark chocolate 🍫🙈!!!! (Haven’t tested what yummy Egyptian mangos will do 🥭) Any tips on what’s a great snack? 

4- What’s in your vitamins? My vitamin gummies apparently have enough sugar to spike my glucose 😔.  I am trying to transition to pills, but I must admit I haven’t been as consistent.  

5- Travel totally messes me up 😫 often, I will carry nuts and chocolate .. see my June 5th data with spikes everywhere !🙈🤦‍♀️🙈🤦‍♀️🙈🤦‍♀️🙈 .. I need to figure out how to travel w olives or something healthy like that :) airport food isn’t helpful either. Any suggestions ? 

6- Better form factor! I have to say the current sensors on the market are so unsexy and many people assumed there’s something wrong with me! I wish the sensor were more compact and discrete. I imagine that will happen as CGMs become more mainstream (in the same way that fitness and sleep trackers like WHOOP or ŌURA ring have become ubiquitous).

7- Multimodal please! How cool would it be if my CGM integrated with my whoop and other sensors like NiX. Imagine if we each had a personalized #AI health companion. This is the future and I know of at least one startup that is working on that .. 

So have you used a CGM? If yes, what have you learnt? What are some #human sensing startups that are worth looking into?

prompt: Write a LinkedIn post that discusses a significant career transition, while expressing gratitude for past accomplishments and excitement for future endeavors. The post should mention the sale of a company called Affectiva to Smart Eye, the achievement of pioneering Emotion AI in various industries, and describe continued support for Smart Eye as an advisor. Highlight current trends and opportunities in AI, emphasize the importance of diversity, ethics, and mission-driven impact, and mention personal aspirations in AI, human sensing, health & wellness, and next-gen human-machine interfaces. Close with a reflection on the meaningful Arabic word "Al Fattah" and its significance for the upcoming year, expressing hope for new opportunities and successes. Include hashtags like #tech and others relevant to the context.
output:
New year. New adventure… In June 2021, I sold my company Affectiva to Smart Eye. 

Now, after two and a half years, it is time to explore my next adventure. Starting Feb 1st, I am transitioning out of my full time role at Smart Eye to focus on my next adventure. I will continue to support Smart Eye as an advisor, helping with the company’s strategy and AI roadmap.

I am so proud of the Smart Eye and Affectiva team and what we have accomplished together. I am grateful. 

We pioneered the #tech category of Emotion AI and brought it to market in several industries. We continue to be market leaders in automotive with more than 300 design wins from 21 car manufacturers. Our latest demos at CES showcased what the future of automotive and human-machine interfaces looks like, marrying Gen AI with Emotion AI. I am rooting for Smart Eye and look forward to seeing continued growth and success. 

So what’s next for me? It is such an exciting time in AI. The next trillion dollar companies will be AI-driven. We are seeing rapid and transformative innovation in AI. At the same time, there’s an increased need for regulation and more diversity in AI. It is an amazing time to start AI companies. Above all, it's become clear to me that the companies that will matter will have impact and be mission-driven. 

I am undecided what form my next adventure could take. Maybe I will build a new company or join / raise a fund to back amazing (female!) founders. I am still on a mission to humanize technology while advocating for diversity, ethics and impact in AI. 

I suspect that my next endeavor will be at the intersection of AI, human sensing, health & wellness, and next gen human-machine interfaces.

I am allowing myself the time to explore what I want to do next and who I want to build my next new thing with. 

My word for this year is Al Fattah (الْفَتَّاحُ), inspired from one of the 99 names of Allah and Arabic for The Opener, The Revealer, and The Granter of Success. The word’s root fa-ta-ha means to open, which in Arabic, has multiple meanings. In 2024, I look forward to new doors being opened. I look forward to co-creating new possibilities with people I respect and admire (and love working with!). I look forward to creating impact at scale.

prompt: Generate a LinkedIn post that explains the concept and significance of tool use in AI agentic workflows, particularly in the context of LLMs (Large Language Models). Discuss how tool use goes beyond simple tasks like web search and code execution, and highlight its expanded capabilities. Mention how developers are using functions to perform various tasks, the role of large multimodal models (LMMs), and the evolution of tool use in LLMs. Include references to recent works and articles for further reading on the topic. End with a nod to the previous post about Reflection as a complementary design pattern.
output:
Tool use, in which an LLM is given functions it can request to call for gathering information, taking action, or manipulating data, is a key design pattern of AI agentic workflows. You may be familiar with LLM-based systems that can perform web search or execute code; some large consumer-facing LLMs already incorporate these features. But tool use goes well beyond this. 

If you prompt an online LLM-based chat system, “What is the best coffee maker according to reviewers?”, it might decide to use web search to gain context. Early on, LLM developers realized that relying only on a transformer to generate output tokens is limiting, and that giving an LLM a web search tool lets it do much more. With such a tool, an LLM is either fine-tuned or prompted (perhaps with few-shot prompting) to generate a special string like {tool: web-search, query: "coffee maker reviews"} to request calling a search engine. (The exact format of the string depends on the implementation.) A post-processing step then looks for strings like these, calls the web search function with the relevant parameters when it finds one, and passes the result back to the LLM as additional input context.

Similarly, if you ask, “If I invest $100 at compound 7% interest for 12 years, what do I have at the end?”, the LLM might use a Python execution tool to compute 100 * (1+0.07)**12. The LLM might generate a string like this: {tool: python-interpreter, code: "100 * (1+0.07)**12"}.

But tool use now goes much further. Developers are using functions to perform search (web, Wikipedia, arXiv, etc.), interface with productivity tools (email, calendar, etc.), generate or interpret images, and more. We can prompt an LLM using context that gives detailed descriptions of many functions plus information about their arguments. And we’d expect the LLM to automatically choose the right function to call to do a job.

Before widespread availability of large multimodal models (LMMs) like LLaVa, GPT-4V, and Gemini, LLMs could not process images directly, so a lot of early work on tool use was carried out by the computer vision community. At that time, the only way for an LLM-based system to manipulate images was by calling a function to, say, carry out object recognition or some other function. Since then, tool use has exploded. GPT-4’s function calling, released last year, was a significant step toward general-purpose tool use. Now, more and more LLMs are being developed to similarly be facile with tool use.

To learn more, I recommend:
- Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs, Patil et al. (2023)
- MM-REACT: Prompting ChatGPT for Multimodal Reasoning and Action, Yang et al. (2023)
- Efficient Tool Use with Chain-of-Abstraction Reasoning, Gao et al. (2024)

Both Tool Use and Reflection, which I posted about last week, are design patterns that I can get to work fairly reliably, and are well worth learning about. 

[Original: https://lnkd.in/gw-EwcTB ]

prompt: Write a LinkedIn post expressing concern about the proposed California regulation SB 1047, specifically its negative implications for open source and AI innovation. Highlight that the legislation mistakenly targets AI technology rather than AI applications, failing to effectively enhance AI safety. Emphasize that alternative safety measures, such as banning nonconsensual deepfake pornography and standardizing content identification, would be more beneficial. Discuss how SB 1047's complex reporting requirements and vague language create uncertainty for developers, especially concerning certification of compliance under penalty of perjury. Explain that the law's demands on developers to predict and mitigate potential AI harms and misuse are unreasonable, given the current uncertainty among leading AI researchers. End by providing a link to the latest draft of the law for readers to review.
output:
I continue to be alarmed at the progress of proposed California regulation SB 1047 and the attack it represents on open source and more broadly on AI innovation. As I wrote previously, this proposed law makes a fundamental mistake of regulating AI technology instead of AI applications, and thus would fail to make AI meaningfully safer. I’d like to explain why the specific mechanisms of SB 1047 are so pernicious to open source.

To be clear, there are routes that regulators should pursue to improve safety. For example, I would welcome outlawing nonconsensual deepfake pornography, standardizing watermarking and fingerprinting to identify generated content, and investing more in red teaming and other safety research. Unfortunately, the proposed bill pursues a less beneficial and more harmful path.

SB 1047’s purported goal is to ensure safety of AI models. It puts in place complex reporting requirements for developers who fine-tune models or develop models that cost more than $100 million to train. It is a vague, ambiguous law that imposes significant penalties for violations, creating a huge gray zone in which developers can’t be sure how to avoid breaking the law. This will paralyze many teams.

You can read the latest draft of the law online. I’ve read through it carefully, and I find it ambiguous and very hard to follow.

Developers who try to navigate the law’s complex requirements face what feels like a huge personal risk. It requires that developers submit, under penalty of perjury, a certification of compliance with the requirements of the law. But when the requirements are complex, hard to understand, and can even shift according to the whims of an unelected body (more on this below), how do we ensure we are in compliance?

For example, the certification must include many different sections. One is an analysis of “the nature and magnitude of critical harms … the model might reasonably cause or enable.” But given that even leading AI researchers aren’t sure what harms models might cause or enable, how is a team of developers supposed to figure this out and declare — under penalty of perjury — that they meet this requirement?

Further, some developers will be required to implement “protections to prevent … misuse of, or unsafe post-training modifications of, the covered model and all covered model derivatives … that are appropriate in light of the risks associated with the covered model, including from advanced persistent threats or other sophisticated actors.” Even leading AI researchers don’t agree on how best to “protect” AI models against these supposed risks, or what would be “appropriate.” So how are developers supposed to figure out how to comply with this requirement?

[Reached LinkedIn length limit. Rest of text here: https://lnkd.in/g_NhQpni ]

prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post discussing the following points in detail:

1. Mention the increasing demand for more compute and data to train large foundation models. Highlight that the need for compute is equally pressing for inference on these models.

2. Draw on your experience leading teams at Google, Baidu, and Stanford that focused on scaling up deep learning. Share how semiconductor makers, data center operators, and researchers frequently asked you if AI would continue to benefit from more compute, akin to the diminishing returns seen with CPU performance for desktop workloads.

3. Describe your consistent response, encouraging them to keep scaling up, noting that machine learning engineers generally never feel they have enough compute.

4. State that this prediction has been accurate so far and that further benefits from faster and higher volumes of inference are still untapped.

5. Explain why faster token generation is crucial, especially for agentic workflows involving repeated prompts, tool usage, planning and executing multiple steps, and collaboration between multiple agents. Mention that fast token generation can help avoid the bottleneck in leveraging existing models more effectively.

6. Reference companies like Groq and SambaNova, which have achieved fast token generation rates, thus addressing this need.

7. Discuss the advantages of faster, cheaper token generation in making the evaluation of models more feasible and less costly.

8. Mention ongoing conversations with Cathie Wood and Charles Roberts of ARK investment firm and their predictions about the rapid decline in AI training and inference costs. Use specific figures for falling costs.

9. Conclude with optimism about the rapid decrease in training and inference costs due to advancements in semiconductors and algorithms, and its positive impact on application builders and AI agentic workflows.

Include a link to the original source as a reference.
output:
Much has been said about many companies’ desire for more compute (and data) to train large foundation models. I think it’s under-appreciated that we also have nowhere near enough compute available for inference on foundation models.

Years ago, when I was leading teams at Google, Baidu, and Stanford that focused on scaling up deep learning, many semiconductor makers, data center operators, and researchers asked me if AI would continue to make good use of more compute if they kept delivering it. For many desktop workloads, like running a web browser, a faster CPU doesn’t help much beyond a certain point. So do we really need faster and faster AI processors? Each time, I confidently replied “yes!” and encouraged them to keep scaling up compute. (Sometimes, I added half-jokingly that I had never met a machine learning engineer who felt like they had enough compute. 😀)

Fortunately, this prediction has been right so far. However, beyond training, we are also far from exhausting the benefits of faster and higher volumes of inference.

Today, a lot of LLM output is for human consumption. A human might read around 250 words per minute, which is around 6 tokens per second (250 words/min / (0.75 words/token) / (60 secs/min)). So it might seem there’s little value to generating tokens much faster than this.

But in an agentic workflow, an LLM might be prompted repeatedly to reflect on and improve its output, use tools, plan and execute multiple steps, or implement multiple agents that collaborate. So, we might generate hundreds of thousands of tokens or more before showing any output to a user. This makes fast token generation very desirable and makes slower generation a bottleneck to taking better advantage of existing models.

That’s why I’m excited about the work of companies like Groq, which can generate hundreds of tokens per second. Recently, SambaNova also showed it can hit hundreds of tokens per second.

Incidentally, faster, cheaper token generation will also help make running evaluations (evals), which can be slow and expensive today since it involves iterating over many examples, more palatable. 

Fortunately, both training and inference are rapidly becoming cheaper. I spoke with Cathie Wood and Charles Roberts of the investment firm ARK, which is famous for its bullish predictions on tech. They estimate that AI training costs are falling 75% a year. If they are right, a foundation model that costs $100M to train this year might cost $25M to train next year. Further, they report that for “enterprise scale use cases, inference costs seem to be falling at an annual rate of ~86%, even faster than training costs.”

I don’t know how accurate these specific predictions will turn out to be, but with progress in both semiconductors and algorithms, I do see training and inference costs falling rapidly. This will be good for application builders and help AI agentic workflows lift off!

[Original text: https://lnkd.in/dJ9tVGh7 ]

- Discuss how the industry has managed without standardized data quality processes and attribute this to the unappealing perception of data quality roles compared to more glamorous data-related tasks.
- Highlight the importance of high-quality data in the context of GenAI and the competitive advantage it offers.
- Conclude by mentioning a recent interview with @Richie Cotton where you discussed this topic in detail and provide a link to the interview for further viewing.
output:
When I say garbage in, you say garbage out. Right?

But who in your organization is actually responsible for data quality? Whose job is it to make sure the data is well designed, intelligently collected, curated, managed, and documented?

I ask many people this question. I get different answers every time. That’s a problem!

We’ve managed to get a long way without standardizing data quality. Without making sure that what goes in is not garbage.

And it’s because we as an industry haven’t done a good job of making this role particularly appealing. You can’t blame people for wanting to do fancy math instead of looking after the inputs to someone else’s fancy math. We’ve sent decades telling aspiring data professionals that fancy math is where the glory is.

But GenAI is fueled by data. That’s what builds your moat. The teams that take the discipline of data seriously will build a much stronger foundation and get much stronger results. 

I talked more about this, and loads of other data topics, with my friend @Richie Cotton. You can watch the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/eqQXXJR7

prompt: Generate a LinkedIn post about a new short course on quantization in machine learning, mentioning its practical applications and the instructors from Hugging Face. Include an invitation to sign up with the provided link: https://lnkd.in/g7yNrG2T
output:
Have you used quantization with an open source machine learning library, and wondered how quantization works? How can you preserve model accuracy as you compress from 32 bits to 16, 8, or even 2 bits? In our new short course, Quantization in Depth, taught by Hugging Face's Marc Sun and Younes Belkada, you'll learn to implement variants of linear quantization, such as asymmetric and symmetric modes, from scratch. You'll also quantize at different granularities (per-tensor, per-channel, per-group) to maintain performance. You’ll then construct a quantizer to compress any open source deep learning model’s dense layers to 8-bit precision. Finally, you’ll practice quantizing weights into 2 bits by packing four 2-bit weights into a single 8-bit integer.

If you've ever run a large open source model on your laptop, you've likely benefited from someone's work in quantization. Come learn how this key technique works under the hood! 

Please sign up here: https://lnkd.in/g7yNrG2T

prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post discussing how AI might level the playing field for different performers in the workplace. Include the following elements in the post: 
1. A brief comparison of AI's impact on junior vs. top performers based on three study findings: 
   - Junior customer support agents were 35% faster than usual, while top performers saw little improvement.
   - The bottom half of BCG consultants had a 43% quality boost with ChatGPT-4, versus 17% for top performers.
   - A law school study found the lowest-skilled participants saw the largest improvements.
2. Mention how AI tools like Midjourney allow even amateurs to perform decently in creative tasks, acknowledging the contribution of the original artists whose work was used for training.
3. Include an alternative UX-based perspective from Ethan Mollick, suggesting that current AI systems may not yet be sufficient to aid top performers.
4. Discuss the difference between individual productivity and marginal enterprise productivity, noting potential pitfalls such as faster submission of unvetted or misdirected code.
5. Conclude with an open-ended question inviting thoughts from the readers on the relationship between AI, skill levels, and performance.
output:
The Great Equalizer: Does AI level the playing field?

A recent newsletter I read argued that AI pulls bottom performers closer to the top, citing 3 studies:
Out of 5,000 customer support agents, junior workers were 35% faster than usual, but top performers saw little improvement. 
The bottom half of BCG consultants saw a 43% boost in work quality using ChatGPT-4, compared to just 17% for the top performers.
A law school study discovered that “the lowest-skilled participants saw the largest improvements” in performance.

And, of course, yours truly can appear to be a half-decent illustrator thanks to Midjourney and the artists whose work was used to train it.

An alternative UX-based take:
“Just because early results for AI suggest that only lower performing people benefit does not mean that this is the only possible pattern. It may be that the reason only lower performers gain from AI currently is because the current AI systems are not good enough to help top performers.” --Ethan Mollick

If AI is the great equalizer, why would it make bad programmers worse at their jobs? What gives?

Because these studies are looking at marginal individual productivity rather than marginal enterprise productivity. Yes, it's possible to write code faster but it's also possible to submit code that's not fully vetted faster... and it's also possible to submit code that solves the wrong problem faster (and to also to write code that creates problems you didn't have before). So before you get caught up in the excitement of individual productivity boosters, do remember that these effects aren't taking the broader context into account. Most GenAI tools are still built with individual UX in mind and not organization-wide design, so do remember to take a pause if you're tempted to draw general conclusions about the relationship between skill/IQ and performance.

What are your thoughts?

prompt: Write a LinkedIn post highlighting the latest important news in AI that hasn’t made headlines yet but is significant. Mention that it's not about Gemini. Inform about Eric Ries (the creator of The Lean Startup) and Jeremy Howard (Co-founder of Kaggle, fast.ai) collaborating to form a new AI lab named Answer.AI. The unique angle of Answer.AI should be emphasized: they are not building new AI models but are instead focusing on discovering practical and valuable uses for the existing ones. Highlight their mission to conduct fundamental research to help organizations unlock AI-enabled use cases and include a quote from Answer.AI emphasizing their focus on application. Share your excitement about their approach, noting the potential business and human value to be uncovered through AI. Conclude by comparing AI to everyday tools like electricity or a screwdriver, emphasizing the importance of intentional application for realizing value. Include a link to read more.
output:
The most important news in AI this week that you haven’t heard about yet.

And no it's not about Gemini.

Eric Ries (creator of The Lean Startup) and Jeremy Howard (Co-founder of Kaggle, fast.ai) are teaming up to create a new kind of AI lab.

It's called Answer.AI.

Answer.AI is a new lab that isn’t aiming to build new models.

But instead trying to figure out practical and valuable uses for existing ones.

Their mission is to do fundamental research for organizations to help them unlock AI-enabled use cases.

“At Answer.AI we are not working on building AGI. Instead, our interest is in effectively using the models that already exist. Figuring out what practically useful applications can be built on top of the foundation models that already exist is a huge undertaking, and I believe it is receiving insufficient attention.”

This is what I’m excited about most: Their focus on application.

My view is that underneath all of the hype and noise around AI today (scroll on LinkedIn for 5 minutes and you’ll see what I mean), there is real business value waiting to be discovered. Actually no - there is real human value to be discovered.

Like electricity, or a screwdriver, AI is just another tool at our disposal.

Only through the intentional application of those tools does real value begin to emerge.

Read more: https://lnkd.in/erkzfaRF

prompt:
Create a LinkedIn post that includes an announcement about a new AI chip, Sohu, highlighting its groundbreaking performance and disruptive potential in the AI industry. Include the following details:
- Introduction of Sohu as the fastest AI chip of all time.
- Mention its Llama 70B throughput of over 500,000 tokens per second, enabling product developments that are impossible on GPUs.
- Explanation that Sohu is the world’s first specialized chip (ASIC) for transformers, stressing that it significantly outperforms traditional AI models in generative AI tasks.
- Comparative performance benchmarks showing Sohu's superiority over H100 and B200 servers.
- Information about recent funding of $120M from various investors, including names of primary investors and notable supporters.
- Description of the remarkable recruitment and partnerships in hardware engineering and AI research fields.
- Future production and customer uptake highlights, emphasizing Sohu's readiness for large-scale implementation.
- Closing statement on the inevitability of specialized AI chips for overcoming current limitations and an invitation to join the team.
- Include benchmark specifics and a congratulatory note highlighting the industry's shift from GPU dominance.
- Mention the historical and contextual significance of the release in the AI hardware landscape.
- Gratitude towards specific individuals who facilitated the opportunity and a callout to partnering investors and supporters.
output:
Meet Sohu, the fastest AI chip of all time.

With over 500,000 tokens per second in Llama 70B throughput, Sohu lets you build products impossible on GPUs.

Sohu is the world’s first specialized chip (ASIC) for transformers (the “T” in ChatGPT). By burning the transformer architecture into our chip, we can’t run most traditional AI models. But for generative AI models, like ChatGPT (text), SD3 (images), and Sora (video), Sohu has unparalleled performance.

One Sohu server runs over 500,000 Llama 70B tokens per second: >20x more than an H100 server (23,000 tokens/sec), and >10x more than a B200 server.

We recently raised $120M from Primary Venture Partners and Positive Sum, with participation from Two Sigma Ventures, Skybox Datacenters, Hummingbird Ventures, Oceans, Fundomo, Velvet Sea Ventures, Fontinalis Partners, Galaxy, Earthshot Ventures, Max Ventures and Lightscape Partners.

We’re grateful for the support of industry leaders, including Peter Thiel, David Siegel, Thomas Dohmke, Jason Warner, Amjad Masad, Kyle Vogt, Stanley Freeman Druckenmiller, and many more.

We’re on track for one of the fastest chip launches in history:
- Top hardware engineers and AI researchers have left every major AI chip project to join us.
- We’ve partnered directly with TSMC on their 4nm process. We’ve secured HBM and server supply from top vendors and can quickly ramp our first year of production.
- Our early customers have reserved tens of millions of dollars of our hardware.

As we hit the limits of speed, cost, and scale on GPUs, specialized chips are inevitable. If you want to change the future of AI compute, please join us at www.etched.com/careers.

(Benchmarks are from running in FP8 without sparsity at 8x model parallelism with 2048 input/128 output lengths. 8xH100s figures are from TensorRT-LLM 0.10.08 (latest version), and 8xB200 figures are estimated. This is the same benchmark NVIDIA and AMD use.) The AI chip landscape, currently dominated by NVIDIA is SO ripe for disruption. Congratulations Etched on your $120M Series A round. I am so proud to be an investor. 

Training AI models today cost billions of dollars (not to mention that training these models consumes the power equivalent of what a country like Costa Rica consumes in a whole year!). At the current pace, our hardware, our power grids, and pocketbooks can’t keep up. 

Etched is changing this. In 2022, they made a bet that transformers (the “T” in ChatGPT) would take over the world. The team made that bet even before OpenAI released ChatGPT to the public - how cool! Today, every state-of-the-art AI model is a transformer: ChatGPT, Sora, Gemini, Stable Diffusion 3, and more. 

The team Etched spent the past two years building Sohu, the world’s first specialized chip (ASIC) for transformers. Sohu is an order of magnitude faster and cheaper than NVIDIA’s next generation of Blackwell (GB200) GPUs when running text, image, and video transformers.

Etched is making one the biggest bets in #AI right now and they are on track for one of the fastest chip launches in history. If they pull this off, every AI product will run on their chips. Excited to be on this journey with the team. 

Gavin Uberti Robert W.  

Ps. Thank you Taryn Southern, Ocean Ventures for bringing me into this opportunity.

Primary Venture Partners Positive Sum Two Sigma Ventures Skybox Datacenters Hummingbird Ventures Oceans Fundomo Velvet Sea Ventures Fontinalis Partners Galaxy Earthshot Ventures Lightscape Partners

prompt: Create a LinkedIn post that shares your enthusiasm for the potential of AI agentic machine translation, provides details of an open-source demonstration project you've been working on, and invites feedback from the developer community. Include acknowledgments for those who assisted you.
output:
I think AI agentic machine translation has huge potential for improving over traditional neural machine translation, and am releasing as open-source a demonstration I'd been playing with as a fun weekend project.

Using an agentic workflow, this demonstration (i) Prompts an LLM to translate from one language to another, (ii) Reflects on the translation to come up with constructive suggestions, (iii) Uses the suggestions to refine the translation. In our limited testing, this is sometimes competitive with, and sometimes worse than, leading commercial providers.

But it gives a highly steerable translation system where by simply changing the prompt, you can specify the tone (formal/informal), regional variation (do you want Spanish as spoken in Spain or as spoken in Latin America?), and ensure consistent translation of terms (by providing a glossary).

This is not mature software. But I hope the open-source community can make agentic translation work much better. Given how a simple reflection workflow already gives decent results, I think there's significant headroom to make agentic translation much better.

Releasing an early software prototype like this is something new I decided to try to see if it is helpful to the developer community. I'd love any feedback on this.

Thanks to Joaquin Dominguez, Nedelina Teneva, PhD and John Santerre PhD for help with this.
